title: Mr Personalities

jokes.text: Tony's comically wrong attempt at basic multiplication: 'three-sixty-five times two, carry the seven'
jokes.setup: Tony needs to calculate 3.65 times 2 for their bar tab
jokes.punchline: He says 'carry the seven' which makes no mathematical sense for simple multiplication
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Tony's mathematical ineptitude is established and this delivers on it perfectly with a nonsensical phrase that sounds mathematical

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Carry the seven? Three sixty-five times two... carry the seven.

jokes.text: Tony: 'Um, they don't ask this kind of stuff on college applications, do they?'
jokes.setup: Tony just failed at basic arithmetic and expressed interest in college
jokes.punchline: He worries that college applications might require the math skills he clearly lacks
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-aware humor that highlights Tony's academic challenges while showing his genuine desire to improve

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think they're gonna let me in? I can't even add! What if they ask me on the application, 'What is two plus two?' I'm gonna write down five and they're gonna know right away I'm not college material.

jokes.text: Tommy: 'Seven dollars and thirty cents.' Tony: 'Sure, you went to college.'
jokes.setup: Tony struggles with simple math, asks Tommy the bartender for help
jokes.punchline: Tony assumes Tommy's math skills are due to college education rather than basic job requirements
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's insecurity about education and tendency to overestimate simple skills

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Tommy, help me out here. What's 15 plus 7?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: See, that's why you went to college.

jokes.text: Character mentioning one personality is a friend and 'another one owes me 50 bucks'
jokes.setup: Discussion of Latka's multiple personalities being harmless
jokes.punchline: Someone has developed individual relationships with different personalities, including a debt
jokes.explanation: Clever absurdist take on how people might adapt to multiple personality disorder in practical terms

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Yeah, well one of his personalities owes me fifty bucks.

jokes.text: Alex: 'I believe you can get through life by common sense, coping, and taking 30 showers a day'
jokes.setup: Alex explains his practical approach to mental health instead of psychiatry
jokes.punchline: The reasonable list escalates to obsessive showering behavior
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke that reveals Alex's own neuroses while he claims not to need therapy. Perfect structure and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I don't believe in psychiatry. I believe in practical solutions to life's problems.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: For instance, whenever I feel depressed, I just go take a shower.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: If I'm still depressed after one shower, I take another one.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I've taken as many as fifteen showers in a day.

jokes.text: Elaine explaining Pokey's funeral: 'Pokey was a rubber toy. With feelings and emotions like any other rubber toy.'
jokes.setup: Elaine defends having a funeral for her daughter's microwaved toy
jokes.punchline: She delivers a mock-serious comparison to other rubber toys' emotional lives
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan delivery that makes fun of parental indulgence while maintaining character warmth

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? It was a perfectly legitimate funeral. Do you know how many rubber duckies never get proper closure? They just sit in bathtubs, emotionally abandoned. At least my daughter's toy got to say goodbye to the world with dignity.

jokes.text: Arlo: 'I'm as finished as a white suit on a sissy who's been wrestling a half-breed in pig slop'
jokes.setup: Alex asks if Arlo is finished with something
jokes.punchline: Elaborate, colorful cowboy metaphor delivered in exaggerated western dialect
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully over-the-top character voice that establishes Arlo's personality through absurdly elaborate vernacular

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Are you finished with that?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Well now, partner, I'll tell ya - it's like when the buckaroo's been ridin' all day long, his horse is all tuckered out, the sun's beatin' down on his weathered brow, and he's got dust in places he didn't know he had places... that there's when you know you're finished, you see? Finished like a tumbleweed blowin' across the desert, finished like the last drop of whiskey in the saloon, finished like a cowpoke's dreams of strikin' gold in them there hills!

jokes.text: Character explaining quitting habits: 'no more promiscuity. No more Doris Marie Winslow giving her phone number when it's listed anyway'
jokes.setup: Character lists bad habits they're giving up
jokes.punchline: Reveals specific personal detail about giving out already-public phone number
jokes.explanation: Nice character detail that reveals both promiscuity and a specific absurd behavior pattern

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: I'm giving up a lot of bad habits. I'm quitting smoking, I'm quitting drinking, I'm quitting gambling.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: And I'm giving out your phone number.

jokes.text: Latka returning to normal: 'Where am I? What... why am I dressed like the poop-kicker?'
jokes.setup: Latka suddenly snaps back to his normal personality while dressed as Arlo
jokes.punchline: His confusion and use of 'poop-kicker' to describe cowboy attire
jokes.explanation: Good character transition moment with Latka's innocent mispronunciation adding charm

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: What is this? Why I am dressed like a poop-kicker?

jokes.text: Oh, no, no, that was just a joke. Joke? Joke, yeah, take a joke.
jokes.setup: Alex asks about his split personality disorder
jokes.punchline: Latka claims it was all a joke, then immediately demonstrates he can't control it
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup for the following sequence where he clearly can't control his personalities

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, about your split personality disorder...

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Was just a joke, man. I was pulling your leg the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Shut up, you idiot! He's lying!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No I'm not!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes you are!

jokes.text: Latka's terrible cowboy impression: 'There is not room enough in this town for the-the-the... the both of us. There is no room. We both-both going to have to go to the other town.'
jokes.setup: Latka tries to prove he was just pretending by doing a cowboy impression
jokes.punchline: He completely butchers the classic western line and logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Latka moment showing his innocent inability to fake being anyone but himself

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: This is a stick-up! Reach for the sky or I'll pump you full of... bullets? No wait, that doesn't make sense. If you reach for the sky, how can I pump you full of bullets?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You're already reaching for the sky, so you can't reach for anything else!

jokes.text: Latka's emotional reaction to royal wedding: 'I cry and I cry every time I think about them'
jokes.setup: Latka is being overly emotional in therapy
jokes.punchline: His tears are over Prince Charles and Lady Di's wedding rather than personal trauma
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Latka's emotional nature and innocent fixation on pop culture events

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.line: Latka, why are you crying so hard? What trauma are you reliving?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Is not trauma... is Prince Charles and Lady Di wedding. Was so beautiful, I cannot help myself!

jokes.text: Latka's childhood: 'We had the wood, we had the bucket, we had the chair. Then when I was eight, my father got a raise, so we moved someplace indoors'
jokes.setup: Doctor asks about Latka's childhood
jokes.punchline: Latka describes extreme poverty with innocent matter-of-fact delivery
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment that's both heartbreaking and hilarious through Latka's innocent delivery of tragic circumstances

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Tell me about your childhood.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Well, we were very poor. So poor, my father, he had to share his shoes with my mother. He wear them on Monday, Wednesday, Friday. She wear them on Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday. On Sunday, nobody go nowhere.

jokes.text: Then when I was eight, my father got a raise, so we moved someplace indoors.
jokes.setup: Latka has described living with just wood, bucket, and chair
jokes.punchline: The 'improvement' was simply moving indoors
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation and timing - the deadpan delivery makes the extreme poverty both funny and touching

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: The 'improvement' was simply moving indoors

jokes.text: Vic Ferrari: 'Somebody blew a bundle at the Dodge City Sears'
jokes.setup: Vic notices the psychiatrist's western-themed office
jokes.punchline: He dismisses it as cheap department store western decor
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment for Vic as someone who sees through pretense with street-smart observations

dialogue.speaker: Vic Tayback
dialogue.line: What's with all this western stuff? The saddle, the wanted posters...

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Matthau
dialogue.line: It helps patients relax. Creates a calming environment.

dialogue.speaker: Vic Tayback
dialogue.line: Calming? This looks like it came from the clearance bin at Sears.

jokes.text: Vic: 'What is this, the "I'm okay, you're okay" corral?'
jokes.setup: Vic learns he's talking to a psychiatrist
jokes.punchline: He combines western theme with famous self-help book title
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that perfectly captures Vic's personality while creating a memorable phrase combining psychology and westerns

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: A psychiatrist? You mean like a head shrinker?

dialogue.speaker: Psychiatrist
dialogue.line: Yes, I help people with their emotional problems.

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: Well, I got problems alright. I'm gonna need 'I'm OK, You're OK... at High Noon.'

jokes.text: Vic about Latka: 'he's all oatmeal north of the eyebrows'
jokes.setup: Vic describes Latka to the psychiatrist
jokes.punchline: Creative metaphor for mental emptiness using food imagery
jokes.explanation: Colorful, original insult that perfectly captures Vic's street-smart personality and creative way of speaking

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: This guy, he's like a donut.

dialogue.speaker: Psychiatrist
dialogue.line: A donut?

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: Yeah, all soft on the outside, but completely hollow in the middle.

jokes.text: About Uncle Bobka: 'Well, you can try, but you have to talk loud. You see, they buried him 12 years ago'
jokes.setup: Doctor asks if he can speak with Uncle Bobka
jokes.punchline: Latka's matter-of-fact delivery about uncle being dead
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with Latka's innocent literalism about talking to dead people

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Can I speak with Uncle Bobka?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No, he's dead.

jokes.text: About Uncle Bobka: 'You see, he had no children of his own to destroy'
jokes.setup: Latka describes Uncle Bobka's treatment of him
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact observation about child abuse disguised as family bonding
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy delivered with Latka's characteristic innocence, creating uncomfortable laughter through perfect understatement

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: My Uncle Bobka, he raise me like his own son. Every day he take me to the woods, tie me to a tree, and we play a game.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: He hide, and I try to find him before the bears do.

jokes.text: Latka's perspective: 'Well, not so bad. Some children never get to see their uncles'
jokes.setup: Doctor acknowledges how terrible Latka's experience was
jokes.punchline: Latka finds silver lining in traumatic child abuse
jokes.explanation: Heartbreaking and funny character moment showing Latka's ability to find positivity in horrific situations

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: I can see how terrible that must have been for you.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes, but you know, it wasn't all bad. My father, he beat me every day, but at least I knew he cared.

jokes.text: Alex personality: 'That's right, I'm a cab driver and I don't mind'
jokes.setup: Doctor is confused meeting someone claiming to be Alex
jokes.punchline: The response mimics Alex's defensive pride about his humble profession
jokes.explanation: Good character mimicry that captures Alex's specific psychology about class and work

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: You're a taxi driver?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yes, I drive a taxi. So what? Is something wrong with that? I am a professional driver. I take pride in my work. Many people, they depend on me to get them where they need to go safely. This is honest work.

jokes.text: Doctor's revelation: 'Alex... he's you.'
jokes.setup: Alex asks who the mysterious personality reminds the doctor of
jokes.punchline: The devastating revelation that Latka has become Alex
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic revelation that recontextualizes the entire episode. Economical, shocking, and character-driven storytelling.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Jeffries
dialogue.line: He reminds me of you.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Me? What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Jeffries
dialogue.line: I mean Latka has become you.

jokes.text: Tony: 'In my high school, they didn't even teach aptitude'
jokes.setup: Tony worries about taking a college aptitude test
jokes.punchline: He misunderstands what aptitude means, thinking it's a subject like math
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy showing Tony's educational gaps and misunderstanding of basic concepts

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I gotta take this college aptitude test.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Aptitude? What's that, like algebra?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No, Tony. Aptitude means your ability to learn things.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh. So I gotta study aptitude? Like, how many chapters is that?

jokes.text: Tony's yearbook: 'That's what it said under my picture in the yearbook'
jokes.setup: Alex suggests not everyone should go to college
jokes.punchline: Tony reveals this exact sentiment was printed under his yearbook photo
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating humor that reveals institutional cruelty while maintaining Tony's likeable character

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what was printed under my picture in my high school yearbook?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: "Not everyone should go to college."

jokes.text: Alex about boxing referee: 'He was the one in the ring who wasn't beating you senseless'
jokes.setup: Alex suggests Tony become a boxing referee
jokes.punchline: Alex's clinical description of the ref's non-violent role
jokes.explanation: Dry observational humor that shows Alex's practical way of explaining things

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You'd be perfect as a boxing referee, Tony. You'd stand in the middle of the ring, you'd separate the fighters when they clinch, you'd count when someone goes down, you'd make sure they follow the rules.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That sounds like a lot of work.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It's actually very simple. You never have to throw a punch.

jokes.text: Louie: 'I kiss the day you went off your nut, Alex'
jokes.setup: Louie celebrates Alex's profitable night
jokes.punchline: He credits Alex's mental breakdown for improved business
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment showing his opportunistic nature and complete lack of empathy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex, you had a great night! You made a lot of money for this cab.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, I tried my best.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what it was? That mental breakdown you had. Customers love it. Keep it up!

jokes.text: Alex to Louie: 'I'd like an outsider's opinion' about the human race
jokes.setup: Alex asks Louie about his opinion of humanity
jokes.punchline: Alex implies Louie isn't really human
jokes.explanation: Sharp insult disguised as a sincere request, perfectly capturing the Alex-Louie dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, what's your opinion of humanity?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Humanity? It's terrible. People are selfish, dishonest, and cruel.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, that's a pretty dim view.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It's realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I suppose you'd know — you're not really part of it anyway.

jokes.text: Louie: 'Funnier than you'
jokes.setup: Alex asks for Louie's opinion on humanity
jokes.punchline: Louie's cutting response that the human race is funnier than Alex
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie comeback - concise, cutting, and character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What do you think about humanity, Louie?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The human race? It's funnier than you, Reiger.

jokes.text: 'A buck and a half for your thoughts. Inflation.'
jokes.setup: Someone offers payment for thoughts
jokes.punchline: Updates the classic 'penny for your thoughts' for economic times
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay updating a familiar phrase, but lacks character specificity

dialogue.speaker: Alex Rieger
dialogue.line: A penny for your thoughts?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: A penny? In this economy? Make it a nickel.

jokes.text: Multiple 'Alex' confusion: 'no, no, not you, Alex, this Alex' repeated
jokes.setup: Two people named Alex create confusion
jokes.punchline: Characters repeatedly have to specify which Alex they mean
jokes.explanation: Functional confusion comedy that builds the absurdity of the situation but becomes repetitive

dialogue.speaker: Dispatcher
dialogue.line: We need Alex on the radio.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Reiger
dialogue.line: This is Alex.

dialogue.speaker: Dispatcher
dialogue.line: No, the OTHER Alex.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Rieger
dialogue.line: Wait, there's another Alex?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, Alex from accounting called in sick.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So which Alex is picking up the fare?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The Alex that's here!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: But WHICH Alex is here?

jokes.text: Alex gives away hockey tickets for concert: 'I should have done that'
jokes.setup: Latka-as-Alex makes a romantic gesture Alex wouldn't think of
jokes.punchline: Real Alex realizes he's being shown up by his own personality
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's self-awareness about his own limitations

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Alex, I have something to say to you. You are my best friend, and I want to show you how much you mean to me.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I have written you a poem. Would you like to hear it?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A poem? Latka, that's... that's really sweet.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Your eyes are like the stars in the night sky. Your smile warms my heart like the sun. You are the most wonderful friend a man could have.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, I... I don't know what to say. That's beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And now I will sing you a song I wrote.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Latka, this is incredible. Why can't I ever think of stuff like this? Why can't I be this romantic, this thoughtful?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Is easy, Alex. You have no personality.

jokes.text: Alex's crisis: 'How come he's smarter than I am? How come he's booking more money than I am? How come he's more fun?'
jokes.setup: Alex realizes Latka is better at being him than he is
jokes.punchline: Series of increasingly painful self-realizations
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating breakdown that captures the existential horror of being outperformed by your own personality

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. Latka's doing my job better than I am. He's more organized, more efficient, and the passengers actually like him.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He's better at being a taxi driver than I am.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He's better at being me than I am.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Oh my God. What if he's better at everything I do?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What if I'm just... not good at being me?

jokes.text: Louie calculating odds: 'Gotta be three to one' for same name, garage, psychiatrist coincidence
jokes.setup: Someone notes the astronomical odds of the Alex coincidence
jokes.punchline: Louie offers completely wrong odds with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment showing his confidence in completely wrong gambling knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Those aren't the odds. The odds of that happening are 10,000 to 1.

jokes.text: 'But this is a fascinating case. I'm a lucky guy.'
jokes.setup: Alex worried about Latka's condition
jokes.punchline: Psychiatrist more excited about professional opportunity than patient welfare
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing psychiatrist's professional selfishness, though not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Dr. Jeffries, I'm very worried about Latka. He seems to be getting worse.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Jeffries
dialogue.line: Worse? Alex, this is wonderful! Do you know how rare his condition is? I'm thinking of publishing a paper. 'A Case Study in Acute Dissociative Episodes.' It could make my career!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: But what about Latka?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Jeffries
dialogue.line: What about him? He's fine. Well, he's not fine, but he's fascinating!

jokes.text: Alex to psychiatrist: 'Don't hog the therapy'
jokes.setup: Doctor asks Alex to share a session with Latka
jokes.punchline: Alex treats therapy like a limited resource to be shared
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan observation that reveals Alex's practical approach to everything, even therapy

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, doctor, if Latka needs therapy too, we'll have to work something out. I mean, I get Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. He can have Tuesdays and Thursdays. We'll split Saturday.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Mr. Rieger, that's not how therapy works.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Look, I paid for this hour. If he wants in, he's gotta buy his own time slot or we timeshare it like a condo.

jokes.text: Alex's self-deprecating analysis: 'she does seem grateful, but I think that's primarily for not inheriting my nose'
jokes.setup: Alex discusses his relationship with his daughter
jokes.punchline: He attributes her affection to not inheriting his physical flaws
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware humor that shows Alex's tendency toward self-deprecation and overthinking

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: My daughter, she's so affectionate with me. You know why? Because she didn't inherit my looks.

jokes.text: Alex's nose anxiety: 'I got a honker here that won't quit!'
jokes.setup: Doctor asks if Alex is self-conscious about his nose
jokes.punchline: Alex's emphatic confirmation with colorful description
jokes.explanation: Follow-up to nose joke that shows Alex's neurosis but doesn't add much new comedic value

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Are you self-conscious about your nose?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Self-conscious? I'm not self-conscious about my nose. I'm conscious about my nose. I'm very conscious about my nose. I'm acutely, painfully, excruciatingly conscious about my nose.

jokes.text: 'I'm starting to realize that my love for life is unrequited.'
jokes.setup: Alex continuing his therapy breakthrough
jokes.punchline: Poetic but depressing realization about his relationship with existence
jokes.explanation: Beautiful, melancholy line that's both funny and profound. Perfect Alex character moment with great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about my relationship with existence, and I realized something profound. We're all just... passengers in a taxi we didn't call, going to a destination we don't understand, with a driver who won't tell us the fare.

jokes.text: Latka interrupting Alex's breakthrough: 'So, we had the bucket, we had the wood...'
jokes.setup: Alex is about to reveal his life solution when personalities switch
jokes.punchline: Latka returns at worst possible moment with his familiar childhood refrain
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed interruption that creates dramatic frustration - callback works as story mechanism

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I've figured it all out. The answer to everything is—

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Oh, hello, everyone! Sunshine, lollipops, and rainbows, everything that's wonderful is what I've been told!

jokes.text: 'must've been placed here by a "put-pocket"'
jokes.setup: Latka finds tickets in his pocket he doesn't remember
jokes.punchline: He creates opposite of 'pickpocket' with innocent logic
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that could only come from Latka's innocent logic and foreign perspective on English

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I find these tickets in my pocket. I don't remember putting them there.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Someone must have put them there without me knowing.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: That's not a pickpocket... that's a putpocket!

tmdb_overview: Latka begins exhibiting multiple personalities again: not only Vic Ferrari, but also a cowboy, and best of all, he becomes Alex! Elaine refers Latka to her psychiatrist, but meanwhile Latka is becoming even more ""Alex""-like than Alex himself: he is a better cab driver and gives better advice to the gang. Alex decides he'd better sit in on a psychiatric session with Latka and Latka/Alex is about to reveal the answer to all of Alex's problems (somehow he has figured out the secret to Alex in the midst of subconsciously becoming him), when he becomes Latka again. This leaves Alex extremely frustrated and after Latka leaves, Alex sticks around to talk to the psychiatrist further.
insight: Four decades of character-driven laughs sustain an 84-point episode without standout moments.
